decisionFrame.goal: Choose the operating model that gives Abivax the best chance of a clean implementation with strong program control and low operator drag on Mike.
decisionFrame.operatorBias: Prefer fewer firms in the room if governance quality stays high. Strong PMO is valuable if it reduces noise instead of adding a parallel management layer.

knownFacts: NetSuite is currently shown as the implementation path on the ERP team page.
CFGI is in discussion as an alternative NetSuite implementor and can also provide PMO support.
KPMG presented a PMO proposal on March 6, 2026 at a flat fee of $350K in the initial proposal.
Mike is concerned about Oracle/NetSuite implementation quality based on past experience and possible Oracle-side changes.
Treasury ingestion is already materially complete: treasury page and sub-pages are populated from Roxandra workbook intake.

decisionCriteria.label: Execution reliability
decisionCriteria.prompt: Who is most likely to actually deliver a clean build, not just sell one?

decisionCriteria.label: Program control
decisionCriteria.prompt: Who owns scope, issue triage, timeline pressure, and escalation discipline day to day?

decisionCriteria.label: Operator load on Mike
decisionCriteria.prompt: Which model minimizes translation work, referee work, and stakeholder herding?

decisionCriteria.label: Role clarity
decisionCriteria.prompt: Can every firm explain, in one sentence, what it owns and what it does not own?

decisionCriteria.label: Control-by-design fit
decisionCriteria.prompt: Which model best protects the SOX / control agenda while implementation accelerates?

models.label: Model A
models.name: NetSuite / Oracle direct as SI
models.summary: Simplest vendor chain on paper: one implementation firm, one commercial owner, minimal overlay.
models.operatorRead: Leanest structure, but only works if you trust Oracle execution quality. Your stated concern is that you do not.
upsides: Single implementor can reduce handoff confusion.
Commercial and scope accountability stay concentrated.
risks: If Oracle execution is weak, there is no independent operating brake system.
Mike may end up acting as de facto PMO and quality filter.
Harder to separate 'implementation optimism' from real delivery risk.
models.bestWhen: Choose this only if Oracle can show a named senior team, strong biotech references, and credible protection against turnover or internal changes.

models.label: Model B
models.name: NetSuite / Oracle SI + KPMG PMO overlay
models.summary: Keep Oracle as builder but add an external PMO layer to drive cadence, issue management, and executive control.
models.teamShape: Oracle SI + KPMG PMO + Abivax core team
models.operatorRead: Most defensible version of staying with Oracle if you want extra control without swapping the implementor.
upsides: Creates an independent PMO spine if Oracle underperforms.
KPMG already knows the selection story and has recent credibility with you.
Can reduce Mike's need to manually police cadence and issue closure.
risks: Three-sided coordination can still get noisy if PMO vs SI roles are fuzzy.
KPMG must be empowered to challenge Oracle, not just run meetings.
Adds cost and another reporting lane.
models.bestWhen: Strong option if Abivax wants to keep NetSuite as builder but does not fully trust Oracle to self-manage.

models.label: Model C
models.name: CFGI as SI or implementation lead
models.summary: Shift implementation leadership to CFGI if they can credibly run the build and not just advise around it.
models.operatorRead: Potentially cleaner than a three-firm model if CFGI can truly own implementation and PMO in one lane.
upsides: May solve the Oracle trust issue directly.
Cleaner than Oracle + CFGI + KPMG all at once if CFGI can carry both delivery and PMO discipline.
Could give Abivax a more operator-oriented partner if CFGI team quality is strong.
risks: Still needs hard validation that CFGI is a real NetSuite delivery machine for this size/scope.
Could create platform-accountability ambiguity if Oracle remains heavily involved.
New path may cost time if role transfer is not crisp.
models.bestWhen: Worth serious consideration if CFGI can prove named delivery leadership, references, and a sharper governance spine than Oracle.

models.label: Model D
models.name: CFGI implementation + KPMG PMO
models.summary: Separate builder from PMO while avoiding Oracle-led delivery risk.
models.teamShape: CFGI SI + KPMG PMO + Abivax core team
models.operatorRead: Can work, but only if KPMG and CFGI have extremely sharp swimlanes. Otherwise this becomes a meeting factory.
upsides: Independent PMO plus potentially stronger implementor than Oracle direct.
Better control environment if both firms are disciplined.
risks: Easy to create too many cooks in the kitchen.
Mike can get trapped between PMO language and implementation language.
Requires explicit RACI for issue ownership, design authority, and escalation rights.
models.bestWhen: Only pursue if Abivax explicitly values independent PMO enough to accept the extra coordination layer.

currentShortlist.name: Model B: Oracle SI + KPMG PMO
currentShortlist.why: Best hedge if NetSuite stays the implementor but Mike wants more confidence and less direct referee work.

currentShortlist.name: Model C: CFGI as implementation lead
currentShortlist.why: Best simplification path if CFGI proves they can really own delivery and governance without needing a third-firm overlay.

antiPatterns: Do not run Oracle + CFGI + KPMG together without a brutally explicit RACI; that is the fastest route to duplicated meetings and unclear ownership.
Do not buy PMO that only reports status upward but cannot force issue closure across the SI.
Do not assume NetSuite platform selection automatically means Oracle is the best implementation operator.
evidenceStillNeeded: Jamal commercial package: detailed scope, SOW shape, staffing, and contract terms.
CFGI delivery story: named implementation lead, delivery references, and PMO ownership model.
KPMG PMO pack: proposed cadence, authority, decision rights, and exact line between PMO vs SI.
Operator test: which model most clearly tells Mike who owns issues, risks, and schedule slippage on Day 10?
